overview: NTP (Network Time Protocol) synchronizes clocks across all network devices — essential for correlating Syslog entries and troubleshooting events across multiple devices.
definitions: Stratum: a hierarchical measure of time-source accuracy — 0 is a reference clock (e.g. atomic/GPS), and accuracy decreases as the stratum number increases.
core_concepts: 'ntp master' makes a device act as the time source for others.
'ntp server' makes a device synchronize from an external source while also being able to serve time to other devices.
protocol_operation: A device configured with 'ntp server [address]' periodically queries that source and adjusts its own clock to match.
packet_flow: Local router -> ntp server [address] -> external/authoritative NTP source -> clock offset calculated -> local clock adjusted -> local router can now serve time to downstream devices.

config_commands.purpose: Synchronize the device's clock from the specified NTP source.
Configure the device itself as an authoritative time source.
Set which interface's IP address is used as the source for outgoing NTP packets.

verification_commands.purpose: Show configured NTP peers/servers and sync status.

best_practices: Configure NTP on every device so that Syslog timestamps across the network can be correlated accurately.
interview_questions: What does 'Stratum' mean in NTP, and why does a lower number mean better accuracy?
Why is NTP considered a prerequisite for effective Syslog troubleshooting?
exam_traps: Believing Stratum 0 is the worst accuracy — it is actually the best (reference clocks).
enterprise_notes: Enterprises typically point all devices at 2-3 internal NTP servers, which themselves sync from external Stratum 1/2 sources.
